The framework is called a Markov Chain.

Part 1: Why Independence Fails and Where Markov Chains Begin
Before you can build anything with Markov Chains, you need to understand the fundamental problem they were designed to solve.
This is exactly the problem Markov Chains solve.
Instead of assuming each step is independent of everything that came before it, a Markov Chain introduces local conditional dependence.

author: lorden_eth
title: COntent : I Built a Polymarket Bot That Buys at 98 Cents and Almost Never Loses
evidence: "I want a Python bot that hits the Polymarket API, pulls all active markets, filters for bond and macro markets where the YES price is at least 97 cents, scores each one on a confidence scale based on price, volume, liquidity, and days until expiry, then sends a formatted Telegram alert for any market scoring above 70/
I want it to remember which markets it's already alerted so I don't get spammed, and I want a web dashboard to monitor it."
Claude pointed out that if the bot runs every 30 minutes and a market stays at 0.98 for two days before settling, I'd get 96 Telegram alerts about the same market.
Every alert ever sent is in the database with the confidence score, price, volume, and Telegram message ID all feeding back into the dashboard.
Claude also built a web dashboard that runs on the same process as the bot, a Flask API behind a vanilla JS frontend.

After the Python bot was working, I asked Claude: "Is there a faster way to do the API scanning?" It explained that even with async HTTP, Python still fetches pages sequentially, one cursor hop at a time.
The docker-compose.yml handles everything: the Python bot, the dashboard, the SQLite database volume, and a health check that restarts the container if it crashes.
The dashboard, I definitely would have skipped.
actionable_takeaways: Convert useful tooling claims into runbook/dashboard/alert requirements, not strategy candidates.
Measure time from raw thread to testable strategy spec and reduce manual steps with safe automations.

author: 0xChaseTM
title: Iceberg: The Cognitive Biases That Make Polymarket a Quant's ATM
evidence: They're running the same biases against the rest of the market.
Favourite-longshot Bias (The Surface)
Multiply that across thousands of degens chasing 5¢ longshots on every market - and you get a structural mispricing that never closes.
Overreaction Bias (Below the Surface)
Retail piles in chasing the move - buying the top, selling the bottom - convinced that this time the news really matters.
Avatar: Fire and Ash opening weekend - Yes at 25.8¢ when the market panicked on early reviews.
This is anchoring bias - and it's the slowest, most profitable inefficiency on Polymarket.
This is end-of-event bias: the systematic mispricing that emerges in the final hours of a market's life.

evidence: Same market price, but less confidence — Kelly automatically scales position size down to 8%.
Full Kelly maximises long-run growth in theory, but in practice:
Model error compounds aggressively at full Kelly
Drawdowns at full Kelly are psychologically brutal even when EV is positive
Most systematic traders use ¼ to ½ Kelly.
At the 0.60/0.40 example above, ½ Kelly gives f* ≈ 16.7% — still meaningful exposure, but far more robust to estimation error.

author: Dipper_pol
title: Information Theory on Polymarket: How 77-year old formula prints million for quants
evidence: He was the MIT professor who invented information theory - the math behind every JPEG, every modem, every compression algorithm on Earth.
Shannon's approach was methodical, derived directly from his own information theory papers.
He was measuring the information content of every bet he took - in bits - and sizing positions accordingly.
Shannon listened, then handed him a Bell Labs paper by John Kelly titled A New Interpretation of Information Rate.
The paper used Shannon's entropy to derive the mathematically optimal bet size for any game with positive edge.
Shannon and Thorp measured edge in bits of information - and built portfolios that compounded for decades while everyone else blew up on a single bad call.
"Bits of information" isn't a metaphor.
A Polymarket position has a measurable information-theoretic edge before you place it - expressed as a real number, in bits.

author: crptAtlas
title: Quant Mentality: How to Use Neural Networks to Outplay Hedge Funds
evidence: In this article I'm going to break down exactly how neural networks extract edge before the trade even happens
PART 1 - WHAT A NEURAL NETWORK ACTUALLY COMPUTES
Most people think a neural network predicts the future.
A neural network learns the expectation function hidden inside historical data.
Here's the insight that changes how you interpret everything a neural network produces:
When you present it with today's feature vector, it has no knowledge of what the last 50 days looked like.
Rather than a single hidden state, the LSTM maintains both a hidden state h_t (working memory) and a cell state c_t (long-term memory).
This is the core of LSTM's long-term memory - the cell state can carry information across hundreds of time steps without it being lost to the vanishing gradient problem that breaks basic RNNs.
actionable_takeaways: Train a tiny baseline model first, then require neural models to beat it after costs and latency.
Label jump events and test reversion only after spread stabilizes and depth returns.
Convert useful tooling claims into runbook/dashboard/alert requirements, not strategy candidates.
Build a fair-value notebook that converts evidence updates into probability and expected log-growth.

hypotheses.thesis: Neural models are useful only if they consume clean microstructure features and beat simple baselines.
hypotheses.first_test: Train a tiny baseline model first, then require neural models to beat it after costs and latency.
hypotheses.risk: Deep models can memorize noisy patterns and produce non-executable signals.

evidence: A 54% directional signal with Sharpe ratio above 1.0, applied consistently across hundreds of trades with Kelly-derived position sizing, compounds into returns that outperform most discretionary traders over a multi-year horizon.
The Kelly criterion gives you the mathematically optimal fraction of capital to bet on any given trade for maximum long-run growth:
Standard Kelly treats it as ground truth and systematically overbets.

author: 0x_Punisher
title: My public bot hit $100k PnL today (how I built it + my mistakes + what is needed)
evidence: Follow it exactly and you’ll avoid the mistakes that kill 99 out of 100 ideas.
> Polymarket API access and a real wallet
Make sure to get API keys plus a live wallet for placing orders.
Just go to Polymarket’s developer portal + create your own wallet.
Real-wallet dry runs surface hidden errors (like NSF rejections) that become valuable signals in your backtests.
Dry-run live with real wallet (zero balance).
Run with real wallet and zero balance until backtest and live match perfectly.
Polymarket CLOB and Gamma websockets + external sources (Binance, Coinbase, etc.).
actionable_takeaways: Compare simple baseline strategies against complex variants with identical data and execution assumptions.
Rank wallets by out-of-sample hit rate, market category, entry timing, and drawdown, not headline PnL.
Rebuild 5m windows and test minute-1 direction, reversal, momentum, spread, and fill assumptions.
Require every candidate to pass data availability, execution realism, risk, and falsifiability checks.

hypotheses.thesis: Simpler strategies and smaller execution surfaces may outperform complex bots by reducing failure modes.
hypotheses.first_test: Compare simple baseline strategies against complex variants with identical data and execution assumptions.
hypotheses.risk: Over-simplifying can remove real risk controls or necessary market filters.
